Course Description

CSCE 442 Scientific Programming

Credits 3. 3 Lecture Hours.

Computational methods fundamental to scientific and engineering applications of computers.

Discussions of computer arithmetic; numerical errors; solving linear systems of equations;

linear least squares regression; solving nonlinear equations; optimization methods and

applications to machine learning; polynomial and spline interpolation; numerical methods

differentiation and integration, quadrature methods; Monte Carlo methods (optional); solving

differential equations (optional).

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 221 with a grade of C or better; MATH 304 or

MATH 308 or concurrent enrollment.

Special Course Designation

None

N/A

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

1. Apply computational arithmetic in real-world scenarios. Understand floating point

numbers, rounding errors, relative and absolute errors, cancellation, and numerical instability.

2. Solve linear and non-linear equation systems, using Gaussian elimination, LU, QR.

Understand pivoting, norms, condition numbers, and apply iterative methods, and the

bisection method, Newton’s method, the secant method.

3. Create and apply optimization techniques, based on the Golden section search, the

numbers, rounding errors, relative and absolute errors, cancellation, and numerical instability.

2. Solve linear and non-linear equation systems, using Gaussian elimination, LU, QR.

Understand pivoting, norms, condition numbers, and apply iterative methods, and the

bisection method, Newton’s method, the secant method.

3. Create and apply optimization techniques, based on the Golden section search, the

steepest descent method, and the Newton method, to solving practical problems.

4. Perform data interpolation for approximation through the application of Lagrange

interpolation, Newton interpolation, Hermite interpolation, and piecewise polynomial

interpolation.

5. Apply numerical integrate and differentiate numerical using forward difference

approximation, Richardson extrapolation, the composite rule, Newton-Cotes quadrature, and

Gaussian quadrature.

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Recommended

Scientific Computing

ISBN:  ISBN-13: 978-1611975574, ISBN-10: 1611975573

Authors:  Michael Heath

Publisher:  McGraw-Hill

Publication Date:  2001

Edition:  2nd Edition

URL for Resource:  https://e6.ijs.si/~roman/files/tmp/M.Heath-

SComputing/scientific-computing-michael-t-heath.pdf

Notes:

Lecture notes will be available on Canvas

Grading Policy

Grading Scale:

A : >=90%

B : >=80% and < 90%

C : >=70%-and < 80%

D : >=60% and < 70%

F : < 60%

There will be five assignments and two examinations, i.e., the mid-term exam and the final

exam. Each assignment has 10 points (10%); it consists of two parts: written assignment and

programming assignment (MATLAB is used). There are points (10%) awarded for satisfactory

attendance. Both the mid-term exam and the final exam has 20 points (20%) each.

The points for all the coursework components:

5 assignments  - 50%

Attendance  - 10%

Mid-term exam - 20%

Final exam - 20%

Total assessment - 100%

Note 1:  The students are required to attend all lectures. An email notice with acceptable

justification should be sent to the TA before each lecture to be missed. A student missing 5

lectures or more without justification will lose up to 10 points for unsatisfactory attendance

(up to 10%), i.e. deduction of 2 points for each lecture missed.

Note 2:  If the marks earned for the final exam are higher than the marks earned for the mid-

term exam for a student, then only the final exam marks are counted,  towards 40% of the

lectures or more without justification will lose up to 10 points for unsatisfactory attendance

(up to 10%), i.e. deduction of 2 points for each lecture missed.

Note 2:  If the marks earned for the final exam are higher than the marks earned for the mid-

term exam for a student, then only the final exam marks are counted,  towards 40% of the

total assessment,  for the student.

Note 3:  Extra points beyond 100% will be awarded for solving bonus problems or exceptional

learning participation.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

With exception of excused absences allowed by student rule 7, the following late work policy

applies:

No penalty for late work if the student informs the TA or instructor before the deadline with

acceptable reasons. Otherwise, late work will receive a penalty of 20% reduction of marks per

day, for maximum 5 days.

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late

work and is exempted from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ) .
